Claim: The model tracks post-COVID unemployment with sub-pp accuracy.
Evidence: V_Full re-calibrated post-COVID UR RMSE = 0.273 pp (±0.023 pp across 5 seeds); baseline calibration gives 0.221 ± 0.007 pp. Correlation with observed UNRATE is 0.79.
Caveat: Result is regime-conditional; covers 2022-01..2026-02 only. Post-COVID is the design target, not a held-out window in the structural sense.
Suggested Wording: Across the post-COVID normalization window the recalibrated heterogeneous ABM tracks U.S. unemployment with a 5-seed RMSE of 0.27 pp (correlation 0.79).

Claim: The model does not reproduce the COVID spike.
Evidence: COVID-crisis window (2020-03..2021-12): UR RMSE 2.82 pp, MAE 2.43 pp, max-abs error 6.07 pp; correlation 0.76; mean sim 4.94% vs observed 7.01%; bias -2.07 pp.
Caveat: Correlation 0.76 indicates the model captures the direction of the crisis even though it under-predicts the level.
Suggested Wording: The model tracks the direction of the COVID-19 shock (correlation 0.76) but under-predicts its peak magnitude by about 2.07 pp on average.

Claim: The model is a normal-regime tracker, not a crisis forecaster.
Evidence: Regime decomposition (S06_01_T01): post-COVID 0.22 pp, COVID 2.82 pp, pre-COVID 0.79 pp.
Caveat: Pre-COVID over-prediction (+0.77 pp bias) is also non-trivial; "normal-regime" should be read as "post-COVID normalization regime".
Suggested Wording: The model is positioned as a post-COVID normalization tracker; crisis windows are reported as stress-regime diagnostics, not predictive targets.

Claim: Worker heterogeneity improves post-COVID tracking relative to separately calibrated controls.
Evidence: Re-calibrated controls (S06_02_T01): V_Full 0.273 vs V_Simplified 0.562; V_Homogeneous 0.545; V_LaborOnly 0.363 pp. V_Full strictly best after each control is independently re-calibrated under identical LHS budget (100/100/100/30 draws × 3 calibration seeds, identical loss, identical training window 2004-01..2017-12).
Caveat: Existence of the gap is robust; the magnitude is not the headline.
Suggested Wording: Holding the calibration protocol and training window fixed, the heterogeneous variant attains a lower post-COVID RMSE than each separately calibrated internal control.

Claim: The heterogeneity advantage is moderate, not overwhelming.
Evidence: Total gain V_Simplified − V_Full = 0.288 pp; heterogeneity-associated share = 94.2%; mechanism share = 5.8%. Old shared-parameter comparison reported 2.116 pp total gain and 97.6% share — the old gap was ~10× larger because controls were evaluated at the heterogeneous ABM's calibrated parameters.
Caveat: This is within-experiment accounting under separate calibration, not a causal decomposition.
Suggested Wording: After separate recalibration the heterogeneity-associated gain shrinks from the legacy 2.12 pp to 0.29 pp, indicating that the advantage is statistically meaningful but quantitatively moderate.

Claim: Labor Search Friction is the most robust standalone heterogeneity channel.
Evidence: Re-calibrated ablation (S06_03_T01): Δ(No-Search) = +0.812 pp; Δ(No-Liquidity) = +0.105; Δ(No-Housing) = -0.036; Δ(No-SLH) = +0.429; Δ(No-Consumption) = -0.025; Δ(Search-Only) = +0.037 pp.
Caveat: This is the only standalone dimension whose ablation cannot be recovered by recalibrating the remaining parameters.
Suggested Wording: Among the heterogeneity dimensions, only Labor Search Friction is structurally hard to substitute: removing it raises post-COVID UR RMSE by 0.81 pp even after the remaining parameters are independently re-calibrated.

Claim: Liquidity and housing standalone effects are weaker after re-calibration.
Evidence: Old raw delta vs new delta (S06_03_T02): Liquidity +0.299 → +0.105 pp; Housing +0.162 → -0.036 pp (sign flips).
Caveat: Liquidity and Housing still contribute jointly with Search (No-SLH delta +0.429 pp); the claim concerns standalone deltas only.
Suggested Wording: The standalone contributions of liquidity-fragility and housing-mobility heterogeneity are largely absorbed by parameter recalibration in their absence; jointly with search friction they remain non-trivial.

Claim: Household mechanisms improve UR tracking but worsen LFPR/EPOP fit.
Evidence: S06_02_T01: LaborOnly variant has LFPR RMSE 2.74 pp and EPOP RMSE 2.47 pp, vs V_Full LFPR 4.83 pp and EPOP 4.76 pp. V_Full beats LaborOnly on UR by 0.09 pp.
Caveat: Trade-off is qualitative; tier-2 weights in the loss are small, so this is consistent with the calibration choice rather than a behavioural finding.
Suggested Wording: Adding the household block lowers UR RMSE by about 0.09 pp but worsens LFPR/EPOP RMSE by 2.1–2.3 pp, reflecting the tier-1-weighted loss function.

Claim: The ABM is competitive with strong forecasting benchmarks.
Evidence: Post-COVID dynamic protocol (S06_04_T01): ABM 0.273 pp; no-change 0.309; ETS 0.309; Beveridge 0.422; flow-based 0.426; VAR 0.663; DMP 0.636; AR 1.611; ARIMA 1.617; Ridge VAR 1.264; hist mean 2.173; drift 6.919 pp. ABM rank 1 of 12.
Caveat: The post-COVID window is sub-pp easy for many time-series benchmarks; the comparison is honest at the post-COVID regime only.
Suggested Wording: On the post-COVID window, the recalibrated ABM is the lowest-RMSE model among 12 dynamic-protocol forecasters.

Claim: The ABM's advantage over no-change / ETS is modest.
Evidence: Margin = 0.036 pp / 13% relative. V_Full seed s.d. is 0.023 pp; the margin falls within ±1 s.d. of the ABM itself.
Caveat: This caveat must travel with every benchmark statement.
Suggested Wording: The lead over no-change and ETS is modest (0.036 pp or 13% relative) and lies within the seed dispersion of V_Full.

Claim: Rolling one-step benchmarks are not directly comparable to dynamic ABM paths.
Evidence: S06_04_T05: rolling protocol uses observed UNRATE at every step; ABM is run as a closed dynamic system with no UR feedback. Reporting rolling 1-step UR RMSE against ABM dynamic path is structurally inconsistent.
Suggested Wording: We report both protocols separately; the headline comparison uses dynamic multi-step forecasts, which is the protocol the ABM operates under.

Evidence: Package E (S06_05_T07): 10 of 14 parameters have top-5 CV >= 0.40 across 5 calibration methods, while predictive performance is method-invariant (CV 5.55%; range 0.214–0.243 pp).
Caveat: This is a property of the M0 / V_Full class; the same diagnostic has not been re-run per recalibrated ablation variant.
Suggested Wording: Across five calibration methods predictive performance is invariant (CV 5.55%), but 10 of 14 behavioural parameters have top-5 CV >= 0.40 and are reported as bands rather than point estimates.

Claim: The model is not a full structural labor-market model.
Evidence: Model architecture (S03_01): no firm agents; no endogenous vacancy posting; no wage bargaining; no product market; no inflation channel. Transitions are E↔U and E↔N driven by exogenous JTSJOR/JTSLDR plus agent-level decisions.
Suggested Wording: The model is a worker-side, prediction-oriented ABM; it deliberately abstracts from firm posting, wage bargaining, and the product market.

Claim: The model is survey-moment-anchored, not direct survey-resampled.
Evidence: Population generation (S04_02): sequential conditional sampling from SCE empirical marginals for Q24_cent50, Q24_iqr, Q13new, Q22new, Q33, rw2, qsp12n, qsp13new; PCA-derived constructs for labor_fragility and flexibility_index. No direct bootstrap of joint SCE respondent rows; no Gaussian copula; survey weights MISSING in current pipeline.
Caveat: Several derived dimensions (e.g., mobility_friction, search_intensity for U) are synthetic; not all are direct SCE measurements.
Suggested Wording: Heterogeneity moments are anchored to SCE marginals and a small set of joint structural targets; we do not directly resample respondent-level SCE rows.

Claim: Future work should add time-aligned survey updating.
Evidence: Current pipeline initializes from a single fixed SCE moment snapshot; SCE moments are not updated through the simulation. This is listed in the limitations and as a future-work item.
Suggested Wording: A natural extension is to update survey-derived moments at the simulation frequency to absorb time-variation in the underlying distributions.

Claim: Future work should add firm-side, geography, sector, macro-financial variables, and stress testing.
Evidence: Model scope (S03_01) explicitly excludes these features; current scope is worker-side only.
Suggested Wording: The model can be extended to a two-sided structure with firm agents, geographic/sectoral disaggregation, macro-financial transmission, and explicit stress-regime experiments.

Claim: The model does not 'predict' the COVID spike in the structural sense.
Evidence: COVID-crisis bias -2.07 pp; max-abs error 6.07 pp; the spike is under-shot despite directional correlation 0.76.
Suggested Wording: We refer to ABM behaviour in the crisis window as a stress-regime diagnostic rather than a forecast.

Evidence: Calibration loss uses 2004-01..2017-12 only, but the recalibration protocol uses independent LHS draws and independent reeval seeds per variant; observed UNRATE post-2018 is never used as a step input to any simulation. We do not claim a fully out-of-sample test in the structural-econometric sense.
Suggested Wording: Evaluation uses independent calibration draws and independent re-evaluation seeds per variant; observed unemployment data never feed into the simulation as inputs.

Claim: The headline 0.27 pp number is regime-conditional, not unconditional.
Evidence: S06_01: full post-2018 RMSE is 1.42 pp; post-COVID alone is 0.27 pp. The 0.27 pp number washes out a +0.77 / -2.07 pp regime-asymmetry.
Suggested Wording: All headline numbers are reported with their evaluation window.
